---
title: "Related Work: Design Science Research"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: IEEEtran
    classoption: [conference]
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    keep-tex: true
    cite-method: natbib
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Related Work

The central finding of this study is a *metascientific* one: across the German
*Lecture Notes in Informatics* (LNI) corpus, research software in software-engineering
(SE) research is reported predominantly as an **output** of the research process
--- a purposeful artifact that *is* the contribution --- rather than as a
**utility**, i.e. data-analysis machinery employed to *test* a hypothesis. That
observation has a methodological consequence we develop in this paper: SE research,
as it is actually written up, aligns more closely with the epistemology of
*Design Science Research* (DSR) than with that of empirical, hypothesis-testing
science. We therefore read the DSR literature not as generic background but as the
frame that best fits our corpus, and we use four contributions --- spanning the
paradigm's foundations, its write-up conventions, its theory apparatus, and its
current methodological frontier --- to make that case.

## The two paradigms, and why the artifact-as-output finding matters

@hevner2004design provide the discipline's short-but-canonical reference for what
DSR *is*. Their framing is directly load-bearing for our argument: they cast IS/SE
research as the interplay of two complementary paradigms --- **behavioural
science**, which *develops and justifies* theories that explain phenomena (software
here is a *utility* in an empirical pipeline), and **design science**, which
*builds and evaluates* artifacts to solve problems (software here is the *output*).
They position DSR between an **environment** (the source of relevance) and a
**knowledge base** (the source of rigour), and offer **seven guidelines**: design
as an artifact, problem relevance, design evaluation, research contributions,
research rigour, design as a search process, and communication.

This two-paradigm distinction is exactly the axis our corpus study measures. When
we find that LNI SE software is coded overwhelmingly as a *result* of the research
process rather than as *analysis machinery*, we are, in Hevner et al.'s terms,
observing a field whose reported output is the **artifact** --- the defining move of
the design-science paradigm. The seven guidelines then become the natural rubric
for asking whether that artifact-producing practice is also *reported* as design
science ought to be (e.g. whether evaluation and problem relevance are made
explicit), which our evaluation-mode and research-process dimensions can test.

## A process model that our lifecycle findings map onto

Where @hevner2004design specify *criteria*, @peffers2007design specify a *process*.
Their **Design Science Research Methodology (DSRM)** is the de-facto standard in
Wirtschaftsinformatik for structuring both the conduct and the write-up of DSR: six
activities --- (1) problem identification and motivation, (2) definition of the
objectives for a solution, (3) design and development, (4) demonstration,
(5) evaluation, and (6) communication --- with four possible **entry points**
(problem-, objective-, design-, or client/context-centred).

DSRM is more than a citation of convenience here: its activity sequence is a
yardstick against which our *software-lifecycle* dimension can be read directly.
Our finding that **project-definition and deployment phases are underrepresented**
in the reported lifecycle maps onto specific DSRM activities that are being skipped
in the write-up --- problem identification/motivation and objective definition at
the front, communication/deployment at the back --- while design-and-development is
over-reported. In other words, LNI SE papers enter DSRM near the
*design/development* entry point and rarely narrate the bookends. This gives us a
concrete, framework-grounded way to characterise *how* SE reporting departs from a
full design-science account, and a nominal structure we recommend for closing the
gap.

## Refining the artifact cycle: the theory that SE reporting tends to omit

@kuechler2012framework refine the artifact-building cycle by asking what kind of
*theory* DSR generates. They distinguish two mid-range theory types: an
**Information Systems Design Theory (ISDT)**, which is prescriptive (*how* to build
an artifact of a given class to achieve a goal), and a **Design-Relevant
Explanatory/Predictive Theory (DREPT)**, which is descriptive (*how and why* the
artifact works, translating kernel theories from reference disciplines). Coupling
ISDT and DREPT --- and relating both to Gregor's taxonomy of IS theory --- turns
the single build-and-evaluate loop into an engine for *cumulative* design knowledge.

This framework sharpens our thesis into a diagnosis. If SE research produces
artifacts (ISDT-type contributions) but reports them as bare *results* --- software
as output, with the problem framing and evaluation trimmed away --- then the DREPT
half of the cycle, the part that would explain *why* the artifact works and feed a
cumulative body of design theory, is precisely what is missing. The
software-as-output finding is thus not only evidence that SE is design science; it
is also evidence that SE tends to under-report the theoretical, knowledge-accumulating
side of design science. Kuechler and Vaishnavi give us the vocabulary to name that
absence.

## The current frontier: validity for artifact-centred claims

Finally, @larsen2025validity offer a meta-DSR synthesis that consolidates references
to the paradigm's shaping authors and confronts a persistent gap: validity is
neither well understood nor consistently established in design science. They propose
a **Design Science Validity Framework** and process model organised around three
high-level claim/validity types --- **criterion**, **causal**, and **context** ---
plus finer-grained subtypes, demonstrated on existing DSR examples (and reflexively
on itself).

This matters for our study in two ways. Methodologically, if SE research is design
science, then its outputs should be judged by design-science validity, not by the
validity standards of hypothesis-testing empirical work --- a mismatch that may
explain why so much LNI software is reported without a recognisable empirical
evaluation. Reflexively, our own artifact (the typology and annotation pipeline) is
itself a design-science contribution, and Larsen et al.'s framework gives us the
vocabulary to state exactly *what* we claim to know and *how* that claim is
warranted.

## Positioning of this study {#sec-positioning}

Taken together, these works let us restate the paper's claim in the field's own
terms. @hevner2004design supply the paradigm axis (behavioural vs. design science)
on which our software-as-output finding lands squarely on the design-science side;
@peffers2007design supply the process model against which our lifecycle-phase gaps
become legible as skipped DSRM activities; @kuechler2012framework identify the
DREPT/theory dimension that artifact-as-output reporting tends to drop; and
@larsen2025validity supply the contemporary standard for arguing validity of
artifact-centred claims --- including our own. The remainder of this paper presents
the typology, its demonstration on the LNI corpus, and its evaluation against these
standards.

# A Research Agenda: Structures and Next Steps {#sec-agenda}

The DSR frame suggests several concrete directions for developing this paper. These
are pointers for the authors rather than settled results.

- **Build the crosswalk explicitly.** Map each of the five coding dimensions
  (research-process position, lifecycle phase, software type, techstack, evaluation
  mode) onto DSRM activities [@peffers2007design] and the seven guidelines
  [@hevner2004design]. A single table aligning "lifecycle phase" with DSRM
  activities would make the "SE enters at design/development and skips the bookends"
  claim visually immediate and is likely the strongest single figure in the paper.

- **Operationalise the paradigm test.** Turn the thesis into a measurable
  classification: for each paper, is the software an *output* (artifact/result) or a
  *utility* (analysis instrument)? Reporting the corpus-wide split --- and its
  trend over time --- converts the qualitative claim "SE is design science" into a
  quantitative headline number.

- **Analyse the evaluation-mode dimension against DSR expectations.** Cross the
  evaluation-mode codes with Hevner's guideline 3 (design evaluation) and Larsen et
  al.'s criterion/causal/context validity types [@larsen2025validity]. If
  artifact-output papers rarely carry a recognisable evaluation, that is direct
  evidence for the "judged by the wrong yardstick" argument and a natural discussion
  point.

- **Name the DREPT gap.** Use Kuechler & Vaishnavi's ISDT/DREPT distinction
  [@kuechler2012framework] to quantify how often software-as-output is accompanied
  by any explanatory/predictive design theory. A near-absence would substantiate the
  claim that SE under-reports the knowledge-accumulating half of design science.

- **Contrast SE with RSE-in-the-sciences.** The companion observation --- that in
  STEM/natural-science RSE, software is typically a *utility* consumed by an
  empirical process --- sets up a clean two-population comparison (SE: software as
  output; NatSci: software as utility) that directly instantiates Hevner et al.'s
  two paradigms and strengthens external validity.

- **Propose a reporting structure as an artifact.** As a constructive contribution,
  offer a lightweight DSRM-based nominal structure (or reporting checklist) for SE
  research software, targeting the under-reported problem-definition and deployment
  bookends. This would make the paper itself a small design-science contribution,
  evaluable by the very standards it invokes.

- **Consider adding a presentation-focused reference.** Gregor & Hevner's work on
  positioning and presenting DSR is the standard companion to @hevner2004design for
  *structuring* a DSR write-up; it would be a natural fifth entry if the paper
  develops the "recommended reporting structure" thread above.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
